On 1/9/17, David Beazley <notifications@github.com> wrote:
> Would you care if the timeout got delayed indefinitely?   What happens if
> the thread operation took a minute?

I'd expect the thread in which timeout is in operate normally.
That is, timeout should be on time. If the result is not ready
I'll walk away. There should be no difference for the timeout
or any other thing there. That's the reason I would be offloading
the the operation (sync or async) to the other thread.
Why would I do that if it still disturbs me?
(I'm ignoring the slowdown effects off the general CPU/core load.)

It would be my fault if the abondoned thread later screws other
things up. If the result is a thing I can not walk away why would
I put it in a timeout? If it is that important then I should sit and
wait for the result without any timeout. I should also protect
the context from any cancellation.

Silly me should have thought of that !

Hi, cool to see more interest in this! My goal with the API is to provide a way to pass the data as raw/flat (numpy) arrays into the viewer and then have some convenience functions or objects to get data from various libraries.

Currently the NGL viewer can handle raw coordinate data but needs the topology as a pdb, gro or mmcif file. However I am refactoring the NGL viewer so that it can handle the topology in a binary format (private branch so far). As to atom selections, in another refactoring effort I am introducing bit-sets to NGL which will allow very efficient transfer of atom selections from libraries to the viewer. My opinion is that the libraries should use their own selection language (though I would very much like to see a common shared language eventually).

I hope to find some time this weekend to cleanup the API and integrate support for MDAnalysis. For the time being I wont be doing MD simulations or analysis myself. But I am very actively developing the NGL Viewer itself and will ensure support for MD data. Also, I am more then willing to share and discuss my ideas on how to push the Jupyter integration forward! In particular, efficient data transfer between kernel and browser will help (https://github.com/arose/nglview/issues/3). Feel free to contact me!

RFC 5054 comparability was not one of my initial goals but several people
have expressed interest in this capability and have sent multiple patches
to that effect.... it's gotten to the point that I'm considering switching
the default implementation over to it. It's been a while since I've looked
at this so I can't recall off hand but I think the padding issue was one of
the first points addressed in the rfc5054_compat branch of pysrp. I haven't
advertised it anywhere so you probably haven't noticed it so, if not, take
a look and let me know if you prefer that approach.

I've been dragging my feet on switching the default implementation over to
that branch because it'd break backwards compatibility. The aggravating
part is that I have absolutely no idea how many people are using pysrp so I
can't accurately determine whether the benefits for switching over outweigh
the potential irritation to existing users....

Tom

On Fri, Jul 31, 2015 at 4:25 PM, Matthew Neeley <notifications@github.com>
wrote:

> The implementation here seems to be incompatible with the specification of
> SRP in RFC 5054 which requires padding in various places that is not
> applied here. For example, in section 2.6
> <https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc5054#section-2.6> on computing the
> premaster secret, the server k value is defined as k = SHA1(N | PAD(g))
> where the PAD function means pad with zeros to match the byte length of N.
> The code here by contrast just computes k = HASH(N | g) with no padding.
> It seems like compatibility with TLS-SRP would be a nice feature to have
> here.

great thoughts, guys!

**on `plt.scatter`**
@jonchar: I think using `plt.scatter` is a cool idea too. In other words, all we have to do is compute the `(x, y)` coordinates for each point, and pass in the `xs` and `ys`, rather than iterate and add patches individually. That will definitely make the underlying code cleaner, and will allow us to separate the logic that draws to screen from the logic that computes `x-y` coordinates.

**on `set_*` methods** 
I'm conflicted on whether or not to remove them explicitly, now that I've heard arguments both ways. On one hand, *right now*, we are using `matplotlib` as the backend. But a declarative API will mean that we should be able to swap in `bokeh`, `plotly` etc. Let's see what your PR looks like, @leotrs. 

**on type checking**
As for type checking... it's the same argument. Right now we're using `matplotlib`, so it makes sense to have `matplotlib` do the type checking. I added those assertion statements in the hopes that the error messages would be more informative for the end-user than a generic `matplotlib` one would be. I guess at this point it's a bit more of a front-end design choice - are we designing for users familiar with `matplotlib` at this point?

I don't really want to encourage people to install dlib.  I'm pretty sure that will lead to even more confused questions.  That's not to say that these instructions aren't useful, but maybe they should be placed somewhere less prominent and with a huge disclaimer that says like "you don't need to install dlib to use it, but if you want to install it as a shared library then do this".  So maybe at the bottom of the how to compile web page.

I'm saying this because I'm really sure that 99% of people who post in the dlib forums don't really even understand what a shared library is or how the C++ compile+link process works. Hell, I've met a lot of professional software developers in real life who didn't understand linking.  The main reason I've always avoided any kind of dlib install process is because I think the step of producing a shared or static library file that's then used later is too error prone for most people.  For example, I've lost track of the number of times I've seen someone compile some part of their code to a library file (or download the file) in some mode or with some compiler or runtime, then try to use that compiled library with incompatible compiler switches, runtime, whatever.  Then they get confusing linker errors or maybe just program crashes and they have no idea what's happening.  


So for example, here are things that will happen if we put this instruction out where most users see it.  People will run it on some linux machine but not know to run `ldconfig`, which sometimes needs to be run after installing a shared library on linux.  Or they will install it on OS X and there will be some similar issue. Does OS X put /usr/local/lib into the linker load path by default?  I'm not sure.  

What's even worse is visual studio users.  The notion of installing a library is just totally broken in windows.  Each version of visual studio has 8 different compile modes (the combination of 64/32 bit, debug release, multi-threaded/not-multi-threaded) and all are incompatible with each other in subtle ways that sometimes show up as confusing linker errors but more often as weird runtime errors.  Practically every single visual studio user who tries to install dlib with this process we are suggesting is going to have a bad experience.  I've already setup the CMake scripts to only generate static shared libraries when you do the install on windows to avoid people needing to use .dlls, which requires updating the system PATH environment variable, which most users will not understand.  Static don't require updating PATH, so at least programs will be executable without PATH variable changes, so at least there is that positive :)

@rsyring I'm reluctant to implement `is_secure` and `allow_secure`, as the term 'secure' is somewhat subjective. In some environments, a plaintext storage is adequate. It doesn't provide security of any kind, and it's the responsibility of the admin to secure the system or environment. The plain text keyring is suitably secure when stored on an encrypted directory or volume as well.

So I try a different take on your issue - does the keyring backend "provide" the security. However, in this case, only the Crypto keyring qualifies (and the other system-backed keyrings don't).

So I struggle to make a judgment on what is secure and what is not.

Although the use of the priority < 1 seems arbitrary, the measure has a documented purpose. A priority of < 1, > 0 is suitable but a priority >= 1 is recommended. I believe that's the best indicator keyring can give about backends without more detail about the user's environment and usage.

I can imagine some solutions here:

One would be to move the Plaintext keyring out of the project altogether and require it to be included explicitly as a separate package if the user desires for it to be available.

A less invasive approach could be to disable the Plaintext Keyring by default, and only enable it by API or environment variable (opt-in), or enable it by default, but disable it explicitly (opt-out).

Another approach could involve raising a UserWarning when passwords are saved in the plaintext keyring (and implementers could suppress that warning in the API or with an environment variable).

The latter two options might prove inconvenient on systems where security of the file is not a concern.

I can imagine other solutions, too. None of them strike me as particularly optimal.

I'm eager to hear your responses on these proposals. I still agree that it's important to address this issue and find a less surprising outcome in this case.

It depends on the error.

For example, with `ConfigDictEnv`, if the error is a `KeyError`, then that error indicates the key doesn't exist in that environment and therefore it should return `NO_VALUE`.

If the error was something else, then that's either a bug in the environment code which should percolate to the top or it's a configuration problem that the user needs to fix which should also percolate to the top. In Python 2, those errors would just percolate as is. In Python 3, Everett will throw a `ConfigurationError` because Python 3 chains exceptions so you get all the information.

Well, git-repo has concept for implicit resolution of the most common use case, which is the upstream ← service remotes. What it means is that I had to make a call in what does the command do when you don't give values.

But that does not mean that in four use case git-repo doesn't help. There, `hurrcorp` did:

```bash
git init .
git hub create awesome --add
git push github master
```

then Bob added some functionality in his own fork:

```bash
git hub clone hurrcorp/awesome
# edits and commits
git hub fork
git push github that-branch
git hub request create
```

then Carol did her own fork on gitlab

```bash
git hub clone hurrcorp/awesome
# edits and commits
git lab create awesome
git push gitlab this-branch
```

then Bob can do

```bash
git lab add carol/awesome gitlab/carol --alone # you do not this remote to be in all
git merge gitlab/carol
```

I looked into this issue today, it seems like there is a problem with the regex used in the function's `nltk.tokenize.regexp_tokenize`.

`blob.py` currently has:
```
    def correct(self):
        """Attempt to correct the spelling of a blob.

        .. versionadded:: 0.6.0

        :rtype: :class:`BaseBlob <BaseBlob>`
        """
        # regex matches: contraction or word or punctuation or whitespace
        tokens = nltk.tokenize.regexp_tokenize(self.raw, "\w*('\w*)+|\w+|[^\w\s]|\s")
        corrected = (Word(w).correct() for w in tokens)
        ret = ''.join(corrected)
        return self.__class__(ret)
```

The regex `\w*('\w*)+` is intended to capture contractions. If you check it on http://www.regexr.com/ it does work (although it also captures ' alone). However, if you try it in python, it always returns blank strings. I guess it does not like the use of () capture group?

The capture group is actually unnecessary, and could be replaced by `\w+'\w+` (which captures all normal contractions) or `\w*'\w*` (which would capture normal contractions plus a word with a leading or trailing '). Either option works in correct().

However, in testing I noticed that its kind of pointless because the correction isn't very good for common contractions ("can't" is replaced with "canst", "we'll" with "well").

If you want to play around with this manually, correct() basically does this step-by-step:

```
b = "I havv goood speling!"
from nltk.tokenize import regexp_tokenize
tokens = regexp_tokenize(b, "\w+'\w+|\w+|[^\w\s]|\s")
from textblob import Word
corrected = (Word(w).correct() for w in tokens)
ret = ''.join(corrected)
print (ret)
```

Anyone have an idea of why the capture group was included, or why it doesn't work in python?

First two happens when you try to get environment of 64 bit process from 32 bit. It happens because psinfo_t  has different fields size for 32 and 64 bit processes. If a new version of `pydicom` pops up without creating a `dicom` alias to the old version (which is, by the way, not much time-consuming at all, if needed I can do that myself), updating the package with `pip` will cause the old version to be unavailable within that environment.
It would probably be still possible to install both versions, but not as cleanly as it would be with my suggestion. By creating the `dicom` package, it would be possible to have installed and managed by `pip` both versions, so that packages depending on the old API may coexist with packages depending on the new API. This does not need to be a permanent solution, nor something that you would need to maintain, but rather a way to give your user-base a way of smoothly migrating to the new API when it suits them better. and at most the environment should be The main issue is that if version 1.0.0 is actually deemed to be stable, the API change should not hold it back from finding its way to `pip` as *it seems that your user-base is willing to move to the newer API, but the unavailability to it in `pip` is holding back the distribution of packages based on it*.

If one is in the need of using the `ImportError` trick but it is not really affected by the API change, then it would be far better to refactor the code to use the `pydicom` namespace, and explicitly access the newer version (it is relatively easy and safe to do so with moder IDEs).

The `requirements.txt` should have been produced by a run of `pip freeze` (because it is meant to be used for the purpose of results reproducibility in local scripts), in which case you would have gotten an exact version matching e.g. `pydicom==0.9.9` and this would give you no problem whatsoever (unless you decide to remove the egg from PyPI, which of course you should not).
The `setup.py` is used instead to manage a package dependency for distribution and there your packaged should appear as `pydicom` if it is not affected by the API change, while should be changed to `pydicom >= 1.0.0` to enforce the use of the new API, or to `pydicom <= 0.9.9` to use the old API , the latter being likely to cause dependency issues if two packages in the same environment require mutually exclusive versions. With my suggestions one would be able to list the dependency simply as `dicom` and then all dependency issues would be avoided.

`grep -A7 -B4 d229af00-6e57-4dcf-9d01-cdd3836e02b7 porechop_FN58_all_reads.fastq`

Which will print out the corrupt read and the one before and after it to compare with.

If a host address is not specified, then SSH will fail with `Bad local forwarding specification`. This is the case with this perfectly valid bgtunnel command for example:

```
bgtunnel 10.0.1.2 --host-port 23456
```
(Generates this SSH command:)
```bash
/usr/bin/ssh -o BatchMode=yes -o ConnectionAttempts=1 -o ConnectTimeout=60 -T -L 60727:23456 10.0.1.2
```

This is because the `-L` argument requires a host address. If you have a good way around this I might look further into it.

Sorry. Cygwin is not supported. There is definitely an issue with using custom FDs (the client program might want to use them for something else), but the choice of custom FDs to redirect standard streams is very much intentional. There are many ways for the client program and its subprocesses to write to fds 1 and 2, and any such write that gets around any sort of filter that you install in-process in the middle of initialization (which is when argcomplete executes) will break argcomplete.

Using stdout/stderr to communicate is a no-go. It may be possible (but is unlikely) that using named pipes is a workable solution. In any case, if you're stuck on Windows, one possible solution is to switch from Cygwin to WSL.

Hi Daniel,

you were faster than I had anticipated (: Thanks for providing the code. As said, pandas was
made for the job, and I consider it superior to my prior solution based on dictionaries,
which has always been a little on the cumbersome side.

That said, we must keep in mind that some people may
actually be using the implementations already, and most people absolutely hate updates breaking
their code. I think that should be avoided whenever possible.

At least in the short term, this implies that the old structure should somehow continue to coexist
with a newer pandas-based line. I can think of a number of possibilities to achieve that. First,
the most straightforward possibility is to create a totally distinct class and mark the other one
deprecated (yet still functional). Second, both lines could coexist in the same class, and third,
there may be a switch (e.g., pandas=False/True) in the constructor, which would, however, to
point to False by default.

While 2 and 3 keep the interface somewhat cleaner by not introducing two classes/options
to handle the same thing, they will make it harder to drop the old line at some point.

In any case, this may be a great opportunity to fully support exoplanet.eu, which provides more
columns now than it did, when I set up the class. This may also a good time for thinking about
some "unified access" to all the DBs. Maybe the documentation of the columns could somehow
be created dynamically to handle changes in the DBs, but that is another thing...

Would be great if you could give this a thought,
Cheers,
Stefan

The two stackoverflow links posted in the first comment have been updated (Coincidentally by me, I am just reading this thread now for the first time.)

When used in any forking web server (gunicorn uwsgi, etc...), processes get forked, thus apacheduler gets instantiated (and possibly started) in each forked worker process. This leads to the "many jobs running" issue.

It is not really apschedulers responsibility to be aware of software built on top of it, that'd break abstraction.

However, as long as you call *start()* on your apscheduler object BEFORE the processes fork, and you use a consistent *jobstore* that has a single point of truth (pretty much anything but "memory"), apscheduler is capable of being run in a multi-process/worker web server environment. (b/c CRUD is performed at one location by all the workers, and jobs are only executed by the started apscheduler...)

http://stackoverflow.com/a/40162246/3923962

Workaround:
```
class DictKeyInvalid(Invalid):
    """Dict key does not match the given schema."""


def DictKey(schema):
    """
    Dict key does not match the given schema.

    :type schema: Schema
    :param schema: Schema with which to validate each dict key.
    
    >>> schema = Schema(All(DictKey(All(str, Coerce(int))), {str: int}))
    >>> schema({'123': 123, 'abc': 'abc'})
    voluptuous.MultipleInvalid: key abc is invalid
    >>> schema({'123': 123, '456': 456})
    {'123': 123, '456': 456}
    """
    def f(x):
        if not x:
            return x
        key = None
        try:
            for k in x.keys():
                key = k
                schema(k)
        except Invalid:
            raise DictKeyInvalid('key {} is invalid'.format(key))
        return x
    return f
```

See `DictKey` docstring for examples. It's not pretty, but it does the job with an understandable error message.

The general approach I've taken with error messages is to be as minimal as possible. For more detailed messages, schema writers should provide explicit error messages. This is because in practice it's very difficult to give accurate error messages for non-trivial schemas. If Voluptuous tries to be too smart it can easily provide incorrect messages. Currently, the messages will almost always be "correct" because they're minimal.

Consider the following simplified (and nonsensical) example:

```python
s = Schema([{1: custom, {1: [2, 3, 4]}, {1: [5, 6, 7]}])
```

You can see that the error messages could quickly get out of hand. Add in custom validation functions, and you could easily end up with messages like `Expected one of {1: <function __main__.foo>}, {1: [2, 3, 4], {1: [5, 6, 7]}`.

So, yes, in theory I agree that error messages could be better, but the complexity of schemas, and the complexity of an implementation that could provide useful messages for those schemas is greater than simply asking schema authors to provide their own error messages. eg.

```python
s = Schema(Msg([1, 2, 3], msg='expected list containing 1, 2 or 3'))
```

You can checkout other discussion in #114, #127, and probably elsewhere.

No problem, any questions you have about it are helpful because it lets me consider things that I might not have thought about. If you have any other questions, please ask them. I want to get as much feedback on the design as possible before committing to it.

Also, you are correct. For representing multiple responses to the same input, the input will have to be listed multiple times. I designed it this way to avoid deep nested lists of responses which might be difficult for developers to read, and more intensive for programs to traverse.

For programmable responses, I usually recommend some form of a customized logic adapter. However, I have seen valid cases where there are, for example, wildcards in statements. So a if a statement is something like: "My favorite color is <color>". In this case, color can be any valid color. These wildcards are something that is well supported by [AIML](https://github.com/gunthercox/ChatterBot/issues/137), but it is currently something that is not well supported by ChatterBot. I will definitely look into the possibility of supporting AIML in the new corpus format, or something similar.

After going through my code again, I thought that some explanations might help you guys as the Bing workflow is quite strange.

There are 4 steps to batch geocode using it, which are:
- preparing the data for upload in their CSV format, which is what the `generate_batch()` function is doing.
- upload that file to their API, which will in return give you an ID to check if that job has finished. This ID is extracted in `extract_resource_id()`.
- check if their service has finished processing the data. The `is_job_done()` function takes care of that, and is called every 5 seconds (`_BATCH_WAIT`) for one minute (`_BATCH_TIMEOUT`) which is when we consider that the job has failed.
- retrieve the geocoded data through `get_job_result()`.

If you need any other explanations, please let me know @ebreton and @DenisCarriere :)

Well, I patched my copy of the script and it seems to work.
Here's a copy of the code to see if it works for anyone else.

```
import re
import PyV8
from urlparse import urlparse
import requests
from requests.adapters import HTTPAdapter

DEFAULT_USER_AGENT = ("Mozilla/5.0 (X11; Linux x86_64) AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) "
                      "Ubuntu Chromium/34.0.1847.116 Chrome/34.0.1847.116 Safari/537.36")

class CloudflareAdapter(HTTPAdapter):
    def send(self, request, **kwargs):
        domain = request.url.split("/")[2]
        resp = super(CloudflareAdapter, self).send(request, **kwargs)

        # Check if we already solved a challenge
        if request._cookies.get("cf_clearance", domain="." + domain):
            return resp

        # Check if Cloudflare anti-bot is on
        if "a = document.getElementById('jschl-answer');" in resp.content:
            return self.solve_cf_challenge(resp, request.headers, **kwargs)

        # Otherwise, no Cloudflare anti-bot detected
        return resp

    def add_headers(self, request):
        # Spoof Chrome on Linux if no custom User-Agent has been set
        if "requests" in request.headers["User-Agent"]:
            request.headers["User-Agent"] = DEFAULT_USER_AGENT

    def solve_cf_challenge(self, resp, headers, **kwargs):
        headers = headers.copy()
        url = resp.url
        parsed = urlparse(url)
        domain = parsed.netloc
        page = resp.content
        kwargs.pop("params", None) # Don't pass on params

        try:
            # Extract the arithmetic operation
            challenge = re.search(r'name="jschl_vc" value="(\w+)"', page).group(1)
            builder = re.search(r"setTimeout.+?\r?\n([\s\S]+?a\.value =.+?)\r?\n", page).group(1)
            builder = '\n'.join(builder.split('\n')[12:]) #Here's what I changed.
            builder = re.sub(r"a\.value =(.+?) \+ .+?;", r"\1", builder)
            builder = re.sub(r"\s{3,}[a-z](?: = |\.).+", "", builder)
            #print(builder)

        except AttributeError:
            # Something is wrong with the page. This may indicate Cloudflare has changed their
            # anti-bot technique. If you see this and are running the latest version,
            # please open a GitHub issue so I can update the code accordingly.
            raise IOError("Unable to parse Cloudflare anti-bots page. Try upgrading cfscrape, or "
                          "submit a bug report if you are running the latest version.")

        # Lock must be added explicitly, because PyV8 bypasses the GIL
        with PyV8.JSLocker():
            with PyV8.JSContext() as ctxt:
                # Safely evaluate the Javascript expression
                answer = str(int(ctxt.eval(builder)) + len(domain))

        params = {"jschl_vc": challenge, "jschl_answer": answer}
        submit_url = "%s://%s/cdn-cgi/l/chk_jschl" % (parsed.scheme, domain)
        headers["Referer"] = url

        return requests.get(submit_url, params=params, headers=headers, **kwargs)


def create_scraper(session=None):
    """
    Convenience function for creating a ready-to-go requests.Session object.
    You may optionally pass in an existing Session to mount the CloudflareAdapter to it.
    """
    sess = session or requests.session()
    adapter = CloudflareAdapter()
    sess.mount("http://", adapter)
    sess.mount("https://", adapter)
    return sess
```

`sys.stdout = codecs.getwriter('utf8')(sys.stdout)` – don't do this. If you want to create a UTF-8 encoded file, just `open("your_output_file", encoding="utf-8")` and write to it. If you are just printing Unicode and a user of your program wants to redirect the output to a file, it's up to him to set up his environment. Actually you shouldn't even enable `win_unicode_console` in your script, it's up to its user to fix his Python environment.

The thing I may fix is that warning for the case where the redirection occurs. But I have to find out what consumers of readlinehook do when `sys.std*.encoding == None` (which is the case only under Python 2).
It's not about being Linux, UTF-16 has no advantage over UTF-8. But given the situation, why do you expect Python to use UTF-8 by default for interprocess communication on Windows?

It depends on how `PyScripter` stream objects work and what they do. If they are basically wrappers over the standard streams (this is how `colorama` works, it wraps the strandard streams and filters and interprets ANSI constrol sequences) the it would be enough to let `PyScripter` wrap `win-unicode-console` streams instead of the standard ones – as is done in the `colorama` case.
Thanks!

That makes sense, I'll remove MathJax then, should I still keep it in the bower.json so that its fetched when you do npm run bower?

The problem is that Github and the Github API are pretty disparate services, and bower uses the GitHub API. GH doesn't rate limit cloning but it does rate limit API calls for any given IP range. So if many people at Stanford start using bower over a period of several hours, everyone with a Stanford ip could be blocked from using bower for a while. I'm inly bringing it up because I've seen it happen before in production and it's really annoying, you basically need to wait until the window passes and your ip is unblocked to deploy. 
If you feel that the chances of this sort of thing happening are small enough then we can remove bower components from the repo and make that an extra build step.

>

>    -

>

>    Added new npm script for building:

>

>    npm run bower # gathers all bower dependencies.

>    -

>

>    Removed the static/vendor directory, replacing it instead with

>    static/bower_components and static/custom, the latter being for things

>    like custom.modernizr that we can't pull directly from another source.

>    -

>

>    Downside: checking in the bower components take up 233MB in the

>    only two choices to deal with this. a) Don't check in the bower_components

>    just made our repo a ton larger, and there's added slowdown when doing a

>    bower_components for deployment if GitHub is down (as that is where Bower

>    downloads all components from. At jobs I've run into problems where the

>    external IP of the company had exceeded the Github rate limit, so no one

>    could do a bower install for several hours. Don't want this to happen when

>    trying to deploy...)

Also, if you want to DRY up your forms, you can group your fields into mixins like so:

```python
from django import forms
from django.forms.forms import DeclarativeFieldsMetaclass

class UserProfileMixin(object, metaclass=DeclarativeFieldsMetaClass):
    username = forms.CharField()
    name = forms.CharField()
    email = forms.EmailField()

    def clean_username(self):
        # ... some shared cleaning method
```

Then to use as a form/service

```python

class RegisterUserService(UserProfileMixin, Service):
    pass


class UserProfileForm(UserProfileMixin, forms.Form):
    pass
```

```python
from django.conf.urls import url

then I added static media urls and it worked:
```python
from django.conf import settings
from django.conf.urls import url
from django.conf.urls.static import static
from django.contrib import admin

urlpatterns = [
    url(r'^admin/', admin.site.urls),
]

```python
import urllib
doc = db[urllib.quote(unencoded, safe='')]
doc.fetch()
doc.delete()
```

Our primary goal with watchman is to be able to answer questions about how the filesystem has changed with as close to `O(changes)` (or better!) characteristics as we can get, so a polling solution with `O(tree-size)` and at the mercy of the network isn't top of our list.

I do recognize the value for these use cases at small scale, so I'm going to interpret this issue as "add a polling watcher".

I have some diffs in progress that will allow selecting a specific watcher implementation.  Once that lands, it will be pretty trivial for someone to add a polling watcher and make the appropriate configuration changes to use it.

I want to be transparent about that not being top of our priority list though.  If someone wants to take a stab at this, I would happily help them work through the PR.

In general you don't need to provide a `.watchmanconfig` file as things should just work.
Watchman configuration becomes more important as the size (number of files and folders) of your directory tree becomes extremely large.

I'm afraid that the folks that work on watchman don't really know very much about react-native, so if you have questions about that, you're better served by talking to the folks on the react-native project.

Thanks for the input. I have generated the basic set of hdf5 tables for the 20 adjusted models of the NGA East, as reported in PEER 2015/08. I am rethinking the uncertainty model approach and I will elaborate on the problems I'm finding and add a separate comment on it in due course..

For Marco's points:
1. I am trying as far as possible to avoid extending the supported standard deviations, and I don't think it is necessary. The parameters are always the same (tau, phi and sigma) it is simply the flexibility in how the user can control their generation that is needed.

2. I think the strategy for testing will depend on which models have parametric forms. For the adjusted versions only the tables are available, and these hdf5 tables have been generated from those published in the appendix to PEER 2015/08. I can use these same spreadsheet to generate the test tables but this is circular so I'm wondering if that adds value. The tables in the spreadsheets published with PEER 2015/04 could be used to test the original parametric forms of the models as Trevor is indicating. I'm not sure how much additional information PEER will supply as the electronic appendices represent the "official" models.

3. Eventually both the original forms and adjusted forms would be good to have in the oq-engine. As the latter can only be implemented in one way it makes sense to make these the priorities. For testing the table-based GMPEs in general I'm not sure if a testing strategy can or even should be developed. This simply puts the onus on the users to construct the tables correctly, in the same way we would expect them to do for an input model. Perhaps some tools to build the tables from a couple of more user-friendly formats (e.g. NGA East spreadsheets, text tables etc.) could be added to minimise the potential for error in table construction from the users.

Alright, I implemented \~something\~, basically what you suggested, only didn't refactor into an additional method, although it might be necessary now.

It checks if there is a PKESK with the current `keyid` in the message, a decrypts with that if found.
If not it checks if some of the subkeys keyids are in some PKESK, if so, it recursively decrypts with that subkey. If not it checks if there are zero `keyid` PKESKs in the message, if so it tries decrypting them with the current key and returns on first success, then tries decrypting them with the subkeys and returns on first success. If all failed at this point raises `PGPError`.

The way authentication was handled was to first get the remotes via the ``pygit2.Repository.remotes()`` function, and then for the desired remote object ``foo``, assign the UserPass or Keypair object to ``foo.credentials`` before running ``foo.fetch()``. This is the functionality that was changed for 0.23.2, as the changelog specifies [here](https://github.com/libgit2/pygit2#0232-2015-10-11).

What I am wondering is A) why this API change did not preserve backwards compatibility, and B) if there was a better way of specifying the credentials in pre-0.23.2 releases, what is this method. Their is a chicken and egg problem: pipenv needs packages to run but these packages can evolve and make pipenv not working. I feel there is easy solution but to package. PBR has the same problem, upon install it is actually run by the system python so without control of its own dependencies.
I however recommend pipenv to be stripped out of all its vendor packages **only** when packaged and distributed by Linux distrib such as Debian. It can runs its own non reg tests and provide pipenv that runs with the right system python packages, under the control of the Debian maintainers.
One should never install python package with pip install without the —user for this reason. But if one does pip install —user pipenv, the official pipenv with all vendored packages is installed from pypi.
But it requires a lot of motivation and work from the Debian maintainer ! Maybe pipenv can prepare a process/scrip to automatize this.

I've used requests a fair bit, and I find that `verify=False` is more broad than the behavior I actually want.  What I _do_ want is the equivalent of pip's `--trusted-host` variable: a way to whitelist certain domain names such that certificate validation doesn't apply.  I think that a blanket `verify=False` parameter is dangerous when treq automatically follows redirects.  You can make a request of a domain you expect to have a bad cert, but get redirected to something you'd rather validate (say a redirect from an intranet service to the wider Internet).

Whitelisting domain names is also a nice match for treq's level of abstraction, which is generally "browser-like" behavior.  The whitelist would take Unicode (IDN), just as treq accepts in the URL.  I want the API equivalent of a web browser's "I couldn't connect to that site" certificate warning—this would be consistent with treq's other browser-like behaviors.  Engaging a familiar mental model should help developers use the API responsibly.

I also like @cwaldbieser's certificate whitelisting suggesting. This is actually closer to browser behavior than my domain name whitelist suggestion. It's probably more useful on the wider Internet, too.

I'd be happy adding both of these features, as they serve different users:

1. I'm writing a performance testing tool using treq. The HTTP API I need to poke is on a trusted internal lab network and uses a self-signed cert.  It would be convenient for me to just say "I don't care about certificate validation for that hostname". That way I don't have to worry about the certificate changing as the host is re-provisioned, upgraded, etc.

2. I'm developing a feed reader which may encounter self-signed TLS certificates. I want the end user of my software to be able to override certificate errors like they can in a web browser. This certificate shouldn't be valid for any other domain (the feed reader would associate it with a specific feed).

I suppose that in the second case what I _really_ want is the intersection of these features: to be able to add a trust root _for a certain domain_.

Yep, the code is currently Django specific since that's what was existing when I started to port it. That's why the Django support is in ``dockerflow.django``.

The plan is to have a central repo for various Python bits, e.g. a Flask extension in `dockerflow.flask` is next on my TODO list for Re:dash/Airflow. The general idea is to keep the code in one repo to be able to implement the core pieces centrally instead of individually per Python-based framework/toolset.

Alternatively we could have made python-dockerflow only implement the core features (e.g. "get content of version.json from a location") and then have separate packages per framework/toolset (e.g. django-dockerflow, flask-dockerflow, pyramid-dockerflow). I decided against that since it increases the amount of maintenance burden dramatically at the stage, and we can still do that split if really needed at a later stage.

this is how i worked around this limitation in a custom upgrade step:

```python
    portal = api.portal.get()
    role = 'NewRole'
    permission = 'My Permission'

    # add the new role
    existing_roles = list(portal.__ac_roles__)
    if role not in existing_roles:
        portal.__ac_roles__ = tuple(existing_roles + [role])

    # rolemap.xml does not allow to add a single
    # role to a permission and leave the other roles untouched
    # see https://github.com/zopefoundation/Products.GenericSetup/issues/8
    roles = [
        item['name']
        for item in portal.rolesOfPermission(permission)
        if item['selected'].lower() == 'selected']
    acquire = portal.acquiredRolesAreUsedBy(permission).lower() == 'checked'
    if role not in roles:
        portal.manage_permission(permission, [roles] + [role], acquire)
```

```xml

Dieter,

> Out of interest: Are there many cases where numpy and numexpr can yield different results?

Quite a few actually.  One of the limitations of NumExpr 2.6 is its restricted number of `dtype`s, so it often has different casting behavoir than NumPy.  Another general case (which you've found) is in edge-cases in complex math.  The complex functions are all branchless, so they don't handle edge cases all that well.  One potential case that may be of concern for you is that Intel VML doesn't support strided arrays, so I believe that NumExpr falls back to its own internal machinery in that case.  So if you do a really cheap binning operation on an array:

    a = np.random.random(1024)
    a4 = a[::4]
    out = ne.evaluate( 'pi*const/ar**2' )

the behavoir may change.

There is a major revision that I've been working on when I have time that fixes the casting issues by supporting almost all of the NumPy `dtype`s, and hopefully will allow us to link against multiple external libraries and let the user pick which library to use for each expression.

To preface this, it's important to know Sentry is not logging. That also means we aim to integrate *outside* of logging. in Django's case, we hook a signal to do this. If you want to also capture events that happen in logs, you should configure LOGGING to bypass the Sentry handler in the cases that would also trigger the Django error handler (which is likely on in django.request)

If you want to add tags to request, you should approach it from a contextual perspective. That is, use ```client.tags_context(...)```, which should get picked up by the signal handler.

gal = cat.makeGalaxy(index=ind_n, gal_type='real')

- remake the files to store the flux in the original postage stamp, and then make ```bad_ps``` include this cut.  Seems like overkill for something that affects such a small fraction of the galaxies, but I could do it.

```python
@functools.wraps(function)
def wrapper(*args, **kwargs):
    data = flask.request.json

    logger.debug("%s validating schema...", flask.request.url)
    error = self.validate_schema(data)
    if error and not self.has_default:
        return error

    response = function(*args, **kwargs)
    return response
```
The problem here is in the first line of the function. It always tries to read the request as JSON.

In turns of cache reset semantics, the key issue is that a modification to one message invalidates not only that message's cache, but also the cache of its parent, and its parent and so on.

As it stands pyrobuf only notifies parent messages once when a child message is first modified - thereafter the parent already knows the child was modified and doesn't need to be notified again. However, we likely can't use that optimization if we're caching the serialized message, since the parent must be notified if the child has been modified so it can invalidate its cache.
I'm hoping that PR [43](https://github.com/appnexus/pyrobuf/pull/43) fixes this issue.

Hi, in general the current functionality the SDK already supports for 2.x should remain functioning. Since 3.0 is released on this month, I would assume it takes some time for most of the aiohttp users to upgrade to 3.x.

But to be more specific, support of `aiohttp` includes two parts: middleware and client. The SDK right now only provides a `aiohttp` middleware but it doesn't provide patch code for the client. So you can implement a whole 3.x support with new middleware, client/config, or you can just do the client/config and stick with the 2.x middleware for a while.

For `TraceConfig` I think the naming is too general if your intention is just to carry enough information to inject trace header to the outbound http call. Could you check module [TraceHeader](https://github.com/aws/aws-xray-sdk-python/blob/master/aws_xray_sdk/core/models/trace_header.py) to see if it fits your needs? 

Also for client support it involves two things: one is to inject trace header to the call, the other is to capture this call as a subsegment. You can check the `requests` patcher to see how a call made via `requests` is captured and at the meantime has trace header injected: https://github.com/aws/aws-xray-sdk-python/blob/master/aws_xray_sdk/ext/requests/patch.py

Please let me know your thoughts and I'm happy to review your PR and merge it as soon as possible.

Fixed by #579

The introduction of SIP protection since El Capitan onwards prevents even root user to edit files under /System folder (which is where the python framework and its site-packages folder is located). This has made it more difficult to install third party python modules as root on the Apple python (and _rightly_ so).
Apple python has an "Extras" folder on its sys.path which contains various third party packages, most of which are super ancient. Attempting to pip upgrade even with sudo them will fail. 
The system python is there only because it's used by some internal OSX tools.
I'm so looking forward to when the legacy python clock ticks, and Apple will have no choice but remove it altogether, or finally support a modern python (cf. Linux distros).

Hey @brettswift, I'd be up for having a helper for this, though it's a pretty simple use case.  In general I think this could be simplified to:

```
with open(filepath, 'r') as fd:
    return fd.read().replace('\n', '\\n')
```

I don't think there's any reason to catch the exception, since it'd hide any specifics of the IOError itself - might as well let the IOError bubble up directly.  This would also replace the actual newline characters with the string for the newline character, which I think is necessary here:

```
>>> x = """abc
... def
... ghi
... """
>>> x.replace("\n", "\\n")
'abc\\ndef\\nghi\\n'
>>> print x.replace("\n", "\\n")
abc\ndef\nghi\n```

@kennu sorry, I misunderstood, I thought you were reffereing to setting up authorizers locally.

So if I understand correctly now, you are trying to set API Key requirements per path/method from the swagger definition, just like you would in the API Gateway console under Method Request. I see that there is a [x-amazon-apigateway-api-key-source](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/apigateway/latest/developerguide/api-gateway-swagger-extensions-api-key-source.html) API-level property that specifies where to get the API Key from. Now, I did not find a way to define the requirement of the API Key in `x-amazon-apigateway-integration`, but I did experiment with setting this up with the console and then exporting the swagger definition from the stage. What API Gateway console does, is it add an extra `securityDefinitions` that requires the `X-API-Key` header to be present. I then applies it to the `security` of the method:
```yaml
securityDefinitions:
  api_key:
    type: "apiKey"
    name: "x-api-key"
    in: "header"
...
paths:
  /somepath:
    put:
      responses: {}
      security:
      - api_key: []
      x-amazon-apigateway-integration:
        uri: "arn:aws:apigateway:..."
        passthroughBehavior: "when_no_match"
        httpMethod: "POST"
        type: "aws_proxy"
```
If you allready have a custom authorizer for the method, then you should probably use `x-amazon-apigateway-api-key-source: "AUTHORIZER"` and return the API Key in the `UsageIdentifierKey` authorizer response.

## Cross reference to A bookmark
In order to use a bookmark, field codes are used. In this case 'REF' is used, with different switches:
1. Hyperlink, showing text: `REF test_bookmark \h`
2. Hyperlink, showing pagenumber: PAGEREF test_bookmark \h 
3. Hyperlink, showing paragraph number: REF test_bookmark \r \h
4. Hyperlink, showing paragraph number, no context REF test_bookmark \n \h
5. Hyperlink, showing paragraph number, full context REF test_bookmark \w \h
6. Hyperlink, showing above, below: REF test_bookmark \p \h

All these usecases require a `<w:fldChar>` element.

```XML
  <w:body>
    <w:p w:rsidR="00FF4E1D" w:rsidP="00981549" w:rsidRDefault="007F3380">
      <w:bookmarkStart w:name="test_bookmark" w:id="0" />
      <w:r>
        <w:t>Test</w:t>
      </w:r>
      <w:bookmarkEnd w:id="0" />
    </w:p>
    <w:p w:rsidR="007F3380" w:rsidP="00981549" w:rsidRDefault="007F3380">
      <w:r>
        <w:t>Reference to</w:t>
      </w:r>
      <w:r w:rsidR="007B385C">
        <w:t xml:space="preserve"> bookmark text:</w:t>
      </w:r>
      <w:r>
        <w:t xml:space="preserve"> </w:t>
      </w:r>
      <w:r>
        <w:fldChar w:fldCharType="begin" />
      </w:r>
      <w:r>
        <w:instrText xml:space="preserve"> REF test_bookmark \h </w:instrText>
      </w:r>
      <w:r>
        <w:fldChar w:fldCharType="separate" />
      </w:r>
      <w:r>
        <w:t>Test</w:t>
      </w:r>
      <w:r>
        <w:fldChar w:fldCharType="end" />
      </w:r>
    </w:p>
    <w:p w:rsidR="007B385C" w:rsidP="00981549" w:rsidRDefault="007B385C">
      <w:r>
        <w:t xml:space="preserve">Reference to bookmark </w:t>
      </w:r>
      <w:r>
        <w:t>page</w:t>
      </w:r>
      <w:r>
        <w:t>:</w:t>
      </w:r>
      <w:r>
        <w:t xml:space="preserve"> </w:t>
      </w:r>
      <w:r>
        <w:fldChar w:fldCharType="begin" />
      </w:r>
      <w:r>
        <w:instrText xml:space="preserve"> PAGEREF test_bookmark \h </w:instrText>
      </w:r>
      <w:r>
        <w:fldChar w:fldCharType="separate" />

      </w:r>
    </w:p>
    <w:p w:rsidR="007B385C" w:rsidP="007B385C" w:rsidRDefault="007B385C">
      <w:r>
        <w:t>Reference to</w:t>
      </w:r>
      <w:r>
        <w:t xml:space="preserve"> bookmark paragraph: </w:t>
      </w:r>

The TLDR; is that the stateful aspect is a consequence of how the biLM was originally trained. 
 Except for the first few batch, the predictions won't vary that much from batch to batch assuming you are using the same `ElmoEmbedder` instance.  The recommended usage is to load only one `ElmoEmbedder` instance (it holds the internal states) and send all batches through it.  If you are concerned about the non-determinism then run a batch or two though it to "warmup the states", then start making predictions with your data

For example, modifying your code to run the same sentence multiple times, the embeddings are constant after the first batch:

    v2 = np.squeeze(ee.embed_sentence("hello my name is mud .".split())[2,0,:])
    distances.append(sp.spatial.distance.cosine(v1,v2))

print(distances)

Here are some extra thoughts on this topic.

The EVM process will be a loose wrapper around the `Chain` object and the underlying `ChainDB`.  The wrapper should probably be written using all of the nice `async` primatives so that it'll sleep while requests and responses are processing.

I believe most of the inter-process communication is going to be over `multiprocessing.Pipe` connections.  We'll need to setup pipes between all of the processes which need to communicate with each other prior to initializing the processes.  Then, on each side, we'll need something to manage the connection which dispatches incoming requests to the proper APIs and returns appropriate responses to each request.

One issue that we'll need to address is that objects which pass through pipes are pickled for transport.  We should verify that a cleverly constructed transaction or block (or other externally received data) cannot exploit this for remote code execution.  At a glance, the documentation appears to say that it's safe, but we should be **very** sure.

The point I'm trying to articulate is less about what happens in the solver (or whether it is reasonable) and more about us deciding a better way forward for the `blas` package and unifying that strategy across channels and communities. In particular that `defaults` and `conda-forge` should be using the same system to select the BLAS used. Certainly part of our pain is `features`, but a bigger part of it is that we are not unifying on a common solution for this problem.

Just to put out an initial proposal to start this discussion, I imagine it will be similar to the existing `blas` package, but without `features` and without the notion of priority encoded in the version. This way channel priority would kick in as normal. Also users should be able to select an implementation with minimal input from the solver. Though this is very rough. So would be very interested in hearing input from others on how to improve this. Can move it to a new issue somewhere if we would like.

Hi, @dylkot , raising a StopIteration is standard behaviour for iterators, see here:
https://docs.python.org/2/reference/expressions.html#generator.next

```

generator.next()

    Starts the execution of a generator function or resumes it at the last executed yield expression. When a generator function is resumed with a next() method, the current yield expression always evaluates to None. The execution then continues to the next yield expression, where the generator is suspended again, and the value of the expression_list is returned to next()‘s caller. If the generator exits without yielding another value, a StopIteration exception is raised.
```

I am closing this for now as I don't think it is a bug or feature, please reopen if you disagree.

1. I wouldn't put the "Expected blah" message because if you aren't trying to guess the lock for the user (which makes sense) then this sounds like you are. In general, the CLI doesn't try and guess what the user meant to do.  In this case, I should simply get a 404.

2. The help text says that `--resource-type NAMESPACE/TYPE` is valid, but the command doesn't accept it. It should accept the alternate form to be consistent with the other generic ARM commands, so this is a bug.

3. One way to avoid the confusion with locks would be to have different lock commands:
- az lock : Current, all-in-one solution. Could be deprecated.
- az resource lock : Manage resource locks
- az group lock : Manage resource group locks
- az account lock : Manage subscription locks

In this way, the user would know exactly what type of lock he/she is working with. Just a suggestion--not really the point of this thread. :smile:

Changing `Math` to `HTML` makes it work for me using Jupyter 5/master:

```
from IPython.core.display import HTML
HTML(r"""<math mode="display" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML">
 <semantics>
  <mrow>
    <mi>x</mi>
    <mo>=</mo>
    <mfrac>
      <mrow>
        <mo form="prefix">−<!-- − --></mo>
        <mi>b</mi>
        <mo>±<!-- ± --></mo>
        <msqrt>
          <msup>
            <mi>b</mi>
            <mn>2</mn>
          </msup>
          <mo>−<!-- − --></mo>
          <mn>4</mn>
          <mo>⁢<!-- ⁢ --></mo>
          <mi>a</mi>
          <mo>⁢<!-- ⁢ --></mo>
          <mi>c</mi>
        </msqrt>
      </mrow>
      <mrow>
        <mn>2</mn>
        <mo>⁢<!-- ⁢ --></mo>
        <mi>a</mi>
      </mrow>
    </mfrac>
  </mrow>
  <annotation encoding="TeX">
     x=\frac{-b\pm\sqrt{b^2-4ac}}{2a}
  </annotation>
  <annotation encoding="StarMath 5.0">
     x={-b plusminus sqrt {b^2 - 4 ac}} over {2 a}
  </annotation>
 </semantics>
</math>""")
```

I'm not sure what `IPython.display.Math` is supposed to do, but it doesn't seem to pass on the XML code MathJax then operates on.

The other example above (using `<apply>`) does not work as `<apply>` and the other elements are content markup. MathML differentiates between presentation markup (https://www.w3.org/TR/MathML3/chapter3.html) and content markup (https://www.w3.org/TR/MathML3/chapter4.html) and MathJax, as far as I know, currently focuses on supporting presentation markup. A short description on the two markup styles can be found here: https://developer.mozilla.org/de/docs/Web/MathML/Element/semantics. That's why `<semantics>` is optional and you can also pass in something like:

```
<math mode="display" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML">
  <mrow>
        <msqrt>
          <msup>
            <mi>b</mi>
            <mn>2</mn>
          </msup>
        </msqrt>
  </mrow>
</math>
```

This is `<semantics>` content and understood as such by MathJax, but not tagged as such.

```
def add_resource_name(resource_name):
    def wrap(f):
        f.view_class.resource_name = resource_name
        return f
    return wrap

That sounds like a great plan. Various people have been interested in this topic to varying degrees and I'd recommend talk to them first to see what sort of difficulties are to be expected. Especially if integration with other theories is required this may be much harder than one would first expect. However, I think separate tactics for particular fragments would still be a great start and a valuable contribution.

Specifically, I recommend to talk to the authors of these papers:
- Botincan, Parkinson, Schulte: [Separation Logic Verification of C Programs with an SMT Solver](http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1571066109004113)
- Piskac Wies Zufferey: [Automating Separation Logic Using SMT](http://rd.springer.com/chapter/10.1007%2F978-3-642-39799-8_54)
- Reynolds, Iosif, Serban, King: [A Decision Procedure for Separation Logic in SMT](http://rd.springer.com/chapter/10.1007%2F978-3-319-46520-3_16)
- @jberdine and @sishtiaq may be interested too.

As I said in #298 already, the problem is that these layer implementations are not always interchangeable. They implement a different subset of features. Technically there should be no reason for `Conv2DDNNLayer` to exist: Theano can replace the default convolution used in `Conv2DLayer` with the cuDNN one where possible, so just using `Conv2DLayer` should be fine. Unfortunately the default Theano convolution interface is somewhat outdated and does not support things like arbitrary zero padding and same convolutions very well. That is why `Conv2DDNNLayer` exists. Falling back to `Conv2DLayer` simply won't always be possible.

Same with `Conv2DCCLayer`, except in this case the Theano optimizations that swap in the cuda-convnet ops don't exist (yet).

> To me, a good solution would be for there to be a single Conv2DLayer which had an additional kwarg such as backend, which could be, say, 'theano', 'cuda_convnet', 'dnn', etc.

This is the 'clean' solution, but take a look at those layer classes, some of them are monsters already. I really don't feel that we should make things even more complicated by merging them into one. The current situation with separate classes is a pragmatic solution to a problem that, as I said, should be solved at the Theano level.

I think merging them would be pretty confusing as well, as there would basically be two stages of 'backend resolution': a Lasagne stage, where we decide which Theano functions to call to implement the convolution (e.g. we could call `T.nnet.conv.conv2d`, or we could call `theano.sandbox.dnn.dnn_conv`) and then a Theano stage where it which ops to use to implement these functions (Theano might decide cuDNN to implement `T.nnet.conv.conv2d` anyway). That doesn't sound very elegant at all.

> I think it's worth abstracting this out now instead of waiting. 

I don't think it is, to be honest. There are plenty of other things that would be more useful to spend our time on. We would essentially be doing double work as there are plans to fix this at the Theano level, which is where it should be fixed. Admittedly we may be waiting a while for that... but maybe then we should be contributing there instead.

So I checked [the mock that you use for sphinx](https://github.com/Lasagne/Lasagne/blob/master/docs/conf.py#L319-L340). It seems that since we are supporting both backends, we will not need to change that code and it should still work.

Nice, thank you!

> This is maybe at the cost of performance because I feel like doing and undoing things that could be taken care of in those two functions.

I guess the performance impact won't be too bad, but we could measure it. It's nice that the change is so local.

With this change, Theta is applied in pixel space. This means the translation must be given in pixels, not as a fraction of the input size. As [I argued before](https://github.com/Lasagne/Lasagne/issues/782#issuecomment-267324977), this might not be ideal, as it will require a very different value range for translations compared to rotation and shearing. Also it might make it more difficult to process images of variable input size; there's no easy way to specify cropping the top left corner, for example.
So another solution might be to figure out the aspect ratio and scale either x_s or y_s (not both), depending on whether the width or height is smaller.
In any case, the operation space of Theta should be determined by an option given to the Layer constructor. Existing networks should just work as before. So we could have three alternatives for a new `grid_space` or `grid_domain` option: `pixels` (your new implementation), `unit_square` (the old implementation, staying the default), and `unit_rect` (a rectangle with the longer side bounded in `[0,1]` and the shorter side being shorter). I'm not very sure about the naming, but does this seem sensible to you?

Also people may come to rely on it and not notice that things change when they stop importing `lasagne.layers.dnn`. If at all, this should be done on the Theano side (like setting floatX=float32 when using a GPU and floatX was not explicitly defined otherwise).

The second is often used for generic filtering or queryset performance stuff, so soft delete support, or pulling in related tables required to construct the str representation.

PyPy really didn't like the change to PyDecode_UTFx: the UTF8 version segfaults, and the UTF16/32 ones only take a non-const `char *` (and may similarly segfault--I didn't get past the utf-8 segfault).  Instead I just reverted the PyPy version to just a `PyUnicode_Decode` call essentially as we had before, passing the encoding as a string, which seems to work reliably.  I think PyPy also doesn't have the same fast-path advantages as CPython for these encodings, nor the issue from #713, so this seems a pretty reasonable compromise.

  -d ${DOMAIN}

It fails with multiple domains but this is expected because all the authorization hooks are run before the actual check is made with the ACME server, and s_server only serves one certificate at a time.

Here's an example with more elaborate auth hook script. This is actually my original use case scenario with Nginx. It expects that nginx.conf has a `include /etc/nginx/conf.tls-sni.d/*.conf;` directive in the `http{}` block:

```
#!/bin/bash

# Get the DNS name for SNI.
server_name=$(openssl x509 -noout -in ${CERTBOT_CERT_PATH} -text | grep DNS: | sed -r 's/.*DNS:([0-9a-f]{32}\.[0-9a-f]{32}\.acme\.invalid).*/\1/')

# Configure Nginx
nginx_config=$(mktemp /etc/nginx/conf.tls-sni.d/tls-sni-XXXXX.conf)
cat <<EOF > ${nginx_config}

We should specify a strategy for non-settleable channels. Currently, the node will just shutdown without any indication of errors. Users should be aware of the fact that losing the channel state can lock the funds forever.


We could (sorted by difficulty)
1) shutdown the node and print informational message to the user
10) if settle fails, set a flag for such channel and do not attempt to settle it again
....
1000) try to bruteforce the balance hash (this can obviously work only for channels with an empty locksroot and low token decimals)

The objects that represent the state of the application must only be altered through a state change that is logged, otherwise we can't have recoverability. That means the application state must **not** be poked around, so we should only access data through read-only views (or copies) [please note that at the moment this is conceptual and not enforced].

Additionaly, you must have a reference to an object in order to call a method of it, that means if you want to get all the registered token address from the registry that you need to poke on the structure of the NodeState and PaymentNetworkState, this leaks implementation details and increases coupling among different layers of the sysmte, that is unwished for.

Lastly, I don't agree that free standing functions are any worse than methods, moving the functions from the views module to the `NodeState` class is effectively just a different namespacing, that doesn't increase the "discoverability" of these methods.

After reading @konradkonrad's comments and also speaking to him in person I think that in order to reach the required protocol state we need to agree on the following:

1. When closing a channel participant should provide the latest transfer he has received.
2. By closing a channel, the closing party also agrees that this is the latest transfer he received from their partner and acknowledged that **they will not be able** to dispute that in the future. 
3. As a courtesy the closing party can also include the latest transfer they sent to their partner.
4. If (3) has not happened the partner should call `updateTransfer()` to show to the channel the latest transfer they have received from the closing party.
5. After (3) or (4) the channel can be settled. Settling should *first* pay out the party that did not close the channel and only if there is anything remaining should they pay out the rest to the closing party. With such a strategy we can be sure that a [scenario](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/pull/276#issuecomment-264309757) where the closing party tries to cheat by closing with an older state, or with no state gets punished for doing so.


If we are in ageement that the above 5 points constitute the general closing/settlement strategy I would like to proceed in [my PR](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/pull/276) by:

1. Updating documentation to explain the closing strategies and what they mean as we decide here.
2. Making `updateTransfer()` accept only 1 argument as before but with the rules we agree here.
3. Changing the `settle()` function so that it always pays the non-closing party first and only if there is 
    funds remaining pay out the other party.

WRT to these specific datasets. If you have downloaded them locally, there are two steps required to use them in a separate Data Cube. First, the _Product_ they belong to must be added to the local Data Cube _Index_. The specification for DEA products is a little hard to find, but there's [a copy of them available here](https://gist.github.com/omad/f6ab64d851a937d01bebeade4a94e929). Once downloaded they can be added by running:
```bash
    datacube product add <products_document.yaml>
```

Then, they can be _Indexed_, by running:
```bash
    datacube dataset add <*.nc>
```

> I just ran across a case where I used this approach: It is essentially one task communicating updates to a second task, but I am not using a queue, because if the second task is too busy to respond to all updates, I just want it to work with the latest update once it has time.

Hmm. I'd use a queue, for that, adding a `Queue.put_force` method that drops the oldest element if the queue is full.

@Fuyukai hmm... I see your point. Where I come from, I see a queue as a FIFO channel: whatever you `put` you can orderly `get` from other tasks, and they provide fan-in and fan-out. That queues have attached buffers is some sort of an extra feature that makes it easier to handle `put` bursts when you can't consume them fast enough. It's very handy, but it's not their fundamental purpose, as I could attach a buffer somewhere else. So, in my mind, I would rather want someone to explain me why >0-capacity queues would be useful and how to be smart about which buffer size to choose. But of course that's neither the only way to use a queue nor the only way to look at them.

Trio docs say "_You can use Queue objects to safely pass objects between tasks_", so it's pretty neutral w.r.t. buffers. If you think that we should explain why 0-capacity queues would be useful, I guess that means that from some point of view one user might assume that queues are just some kind of data structure (like e.g. `deque`) and not a synchronization primitive. So maybe we could adjust the doc to avert that assumption, e.g. by saying that queues "can optionally have a buffer that is always bounded in size". Or only worry about this in case we re-design things for #497 and maybe call the concept "channel" or something else.

@lsorber o dear, anyways, the reason why this error happens, because NMSLIB l2_sparse and l1_sparse space doesn't like empty vectors, but it, unfortunately, fails to produce a cohere error. Try this script to see:
```
import numpy as np
import scipy, scipy.sparse


def test_l1_sparse():
    index = nmslib.init(
        method='hnsw',
        space='l1_sparse',  # Also fails for e.g., l2_sparse
        space_params=None,
        data_type=nmslib.DataType.SPARSE_VECTOR,
        dtype=nmslib.DistType.FLOAT)
    X = scipy.sparse.random(
        10_000,
        100,
        density=0.1,
        format='csr',
        dtype=np.float32,
        random_state=42)
    minQty = 1e10
    for x in X:
      minQty = min(minQty, x.count_nonzero())
    print('minQty: %d' % minQty)
    index.addDataPointBatch(X)
    index.createIndex()

test_l1_sparse()

```
